attributes.stem: A 30-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 5 day history of ascending bilateral leg weakness and paresthesia. He is starting to feel short of breath and has also noticed similar symptoms in his hands and wrists, which started this morning. Upon further questioning, he reveals that 2 weeks prior to the onset of symptoms, he was unwell with a 6 day history of bloody diarrhoea, fever, nausea and abdominal cramping. On physical examination, he has 2/5 power in the lower limbs, hands and wrists bilaterally, along with areflexia in these areas. There are no sensory deficits found.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely causative organism in this man?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is consistent with Guillain-Barre syndrome (GBS). This is supported by the ascending bilateral and symmetrical motor weakness, along with absent reflexes, paresthesia (seen in 80% of presentations) and a normal sensory exam. This presentation is seen in the most common variant of GBS; acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy. The shortness of breath suggests respiratory muscle involvement, which is a feared complication of GBS. Severe compromise requiring ventilation occurs in 10-30% of patients. Spirometry is used to detect this complication and will demonstrate a reduction in forced vital capacity.

The most common cause of GBS is Campylobacter Jejuni accounting for 30-40% of cases and will precede the onset of neurological symptoms by 1-2 weeks. Patients typically present with acute abdominal cramping, periumbilical pain and diarrhoea which can contain blood. Thus, Campylobacter jejuni is the most likely cause in this case, making this the correct answer.

Clostridium difficile typically occurs post-antibiotic use (particularly after treatment with clindamycin and certain cephalosporins) and very rarely presents with bloody diarrhoea. Instead, it is usually associated with watery diarrhoea as well as lower abdominal pain, cramping, low grade fever, nausea and anorexia. It is not known to be associated with GBS, making this option incorrect.

Whilst Shigella dysenteriae and Salmonella enteritidis present similarly to C.jejuni, with fever, nausea, vomiting and bloody diarrhoea, neither are known to cause GBS, making both of these answers incorrect.

Norovirus, whilst a common cause of viral gastroenteritis, does not typically cause bloody diarrhoea, self resolves within 48-72 hours and is not known to trigger GBS. Therefore Norovirus is also an option that is incorrect.
learningPoints: Campylobacter jejuni is the commonest trigger of the Guillain-Barre syndrome
references: Alshekhlee A, Hussain Z, Sultan B, Katirji B. Guillain-Barre syndrome: incidence and mortality rates in US hospitals. Neurology. 2008 Apr;70(18): 1608-13
Bagdasarian N, Rao K, Malani PN. Diagnosis and treatment of Clostridium difficile in adults: a systematic review. JAMA. 2015 Jan;313(4)398-408
Graham DY, Jiang X, Tanaka T, Opekun AR, Madore HP, Estes MK. Norwalk virus infection of volunteers: new insights based on improved assays. J Infect Dis. 1994 Jul;170(1):34-43
Nachamkin I, Allos BM, Ho T. Campylobacter species and Guillain-Barre syndrome. Clin Microbiol Rev. 1998 Jul;11(3): 555-67
